scenarioStory.history: 19-year-old female college student found in her dorm room by her roommate, tripoding on the edge of the bed in severe respiratory distress. Over an hour of repeated rescue inhaler use with no relief. Has had worsening symptoms for three days after a viral URI. Two prior hospitalizations for asthma this year including one ICU admission with intubation six months ago.
scenarioStory.discussion: Recognize near-fatal asthma and apply MATC Respiratory Distress protocol aggressively. Use CPAP with in-line bronchodilators to avoid intubation. Understand magnesium sulfate as a rescue therapy in severe bronchoconstriction. Apply a modified ventilation strategy to avoid breath stacking in asthma. Use ketamine as the induction agent of choice in status asthmaticus
scenarioStory.course: Arrival — Silent Chest, Exhausted Patient: Near-fatal asthma presentation. Silent chest bilaterally is NOT reassurance — it signals critical airflow obstruction with no air moving. Prior ICU/intubation history and recent rescue inhaler abuse are risk factors for death. Patient is fatiguing and may tire out to respiratory arrest if not aggressively treated. → Default Path — Responding to Treatment: After 10-15 minutes of aggressive treatment (bronchodilators, steroid, magnesium, CPAP), the patient starts to improve. Air movement returns — now audibly wheezing (an IMPROVEMENT from silent chest). SpO2 climbing, RR decreasing, speaking short phrases. Continue therapy and transport. → Post-Intubation Ventilation Strategy: Tube confirmed. The hard part is ventilating — high airway pressures, severe air trapping, and risk of pneumothorax. Low rate, long exhalation, small tidal volumes, and permissive hypercapnia are the rules. If the patient decompensates suddenly after intubation, disconnect the circuit and let them exhale.

patientInformation.patientCondition: Severe asthma exacerbation unresponsive to rescue inhaler

scenarioEvents.name: Arrival — Silent Chest, Exhausted Patient
scenarioEvents.description: Near-fatal asthma presentation. Silent chest bilaterally is NOT reassurance — it signals critical airflow obstruction with no air moving. Prior ICU/intubation history and recent rescue inhaler abuse are risk factors for death. Patient is fatiguing and may tire out to respiratory arrest if not aggressively treated.

scenarioEvents.name: Default Path — Responding to Treatment
scenarioEvents.description: After 10-15 minutes of aggressive treatment (bronchodilators, steroid, magnesium, CPAP), the patient starts to improve. Air movement returns — now audibly wheezing (an IMPROVEMENT from silent chest). SpO2 climbing, RR decreasing, speaking short phrases. Continue therapy and transport.

scenarioEvents.name: Post-Intubation Ventilation Strategy
scenarioEvents.description: Tube confirmed. The hard part is ventilating — high airway pressures, severe air trapping, and risk of pneumothorax. Low rate, long exhalation, small tidal volumes, and permissive hypercapnia are the rules. If the patient decompensates suddenly after intubation, disconnect the circuit and let them exhale.

checklist.title: Recognize silent chest as a SEVERE finding, not improvement
Recognize prior intubation history as the strongest risk factor for death
Administer combination Albuterol 5 mg + Ipratropium 0.5 mg nebulized with unlimited repetition
Administer IV Methylprednisolone 2 mg/kg (max 125 mg) OR Dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg (max 16 mg)
Administer Magnesium sulfate 2 g IV over 10-15 minutes
Apply CPAP with in-line nebulizer and coach the patient to tolerate it
IM Epinephrine 0.3 mg for impending respiratory failure despite optimal nebulizer therapy
If respiratory failure: RSI with Ketamine + Rocuronium
Post-intubation: SLOW ventilation (6-8/min) with long exhalation to prevent air trapping
IV access for magnesium and rescue medications
